instructor.name: Jesus Sanchez Jr.
instructor.office_hours: In office, Tuesday and Thursday 3:00PM-3:50PM or by appointment.

chunks.title: Email Policy
chunks.content: I'll reply to e-mails within 24 hours during the week and within 48 hours during weekends.

chunks.title: Overview / Course Structure
chunks.content:
This is an introductory course on the subject of linear algebra with applications to multivariable calculus.

There will be two in-person lectures per week. Weekly homework will be assigned on Canvas every Friday, which will be due the following Friday. The course has two main objectives which is for the student to learn Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus. We will follow the same sequential order of chapters in the course textbook listed above. There will be weekly homework assignments, two midterms, and one final exam. Below is a tentative schedule for the course.

chunks.title: Textbook
chunks.content: S. J. Leon and S. J. Colley, Math 311: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus, custom edition for TAMU by Pearson Publishing

chunks.title: Tentative Schedule
chunks.content:
Week 1: Chapter 1
Week 2: Chapter 1 and 2
Week 3: Chapter 2 and 3
Week 4: Chapter 3
Week 5: End of Chapter 3 and Midterm I on 2/13/25
Week 6: Chapter 4
Week 7: Chapter 5
Week 8: Chapter 6
Week 9: Spring Break
Week 10: Chapter 6 and Midterm II on 3/19
Week 11: Chapter 7
Week 12: Chapter 8
Week 13: Chapter 9
Week 14: Chapter 10
Week 15: Chapter 11
Week 16: Chapter 11

chunks.title: Grading policy / Midterms / Final Exam / Homework / Course Policies (Calculators)
chunks.content:
The grading for the course will be split as follows: 40% will be assigned to weekly homework, 25% for the midterm exams, and 35% for the final exam.

There will be two in-class midterms. The first exam will cover topics from chapters 1 through 3 and will be on Feb 12. The second exam will cover material from chapters 4 through 6 and will be on Mar 19.

The final exam will be held on May 1st, 1:00PM-3:00PM. The exam will be cumulative covering chapters 1-11 of the course textbook.

All homework will be assigned on a weekly basis and will be submitted through gradescope. The homework will consist of selected problems from the book with occasional problems written by the instructor. Assignments and their due dates will be posted on Canvas under the gradescope tab in Canvas. Collaboration on homework assignments is welcome in this course, however each student is responsible for writing and submitting their own homework assignment.

No calculators or calculation software will be allowed or required during exams.

chunks.title: Learning Objectives: Desired computational skills from completing this course: / Desired theoretical/conceptual skills from completing this course:
chunks.content:
• Be able to solve linear systems of equations using various techniques
• Be able to calculate the determinant of a matrix Understand how eigenvalues play a role in the solving of linear systems and in the study of critical points of multivariable functions
• Understand techniques for solving [systems of] ordinary differential equations that rely on concepts of linear algebra
• Find and classify critical points of a scalar-valued function
• Be able to parameterize curves, surfaces, and three-dimensional solid regions
• Be able to calculate line and surface integrals, and judge when it is appropriate to use Green's Theorem, Stokes' Theorem, and the Divergence Theorem

• Be able to translate mathematical statements about vectors and matrices in Euclidean space into statements pertaining to abstract vector spaces
• Be able to use small/lower-dimensional examples as a guide to understand why abstract results may be true in Rn.
• Be able to identify and provide examples of linear transformations
• Prove abstract statements involving vector spaces, inner product spaces, and norms
• Recognize where concepts of linear algebra play a role in rigorously defining and manipulating objects in multivariable calculus

chunks.title: Attendance / Course Policies (Late Homework) / Course Policies (Missed Midterm) / Course Policies (Missed Final Exam)
chunks.content:
Is not mandatory, but encouraged. If you miss a class it is your responsibility to review the topics viewed in class, and you're welcome to come to office hours if you have any questions.

Late homework will be accepted only under exceptional situations.

If you miss a midterm exam due to illness or other extreme circumstances, we will set up a time for you to take a make-up exam.

Verified illness or other extreme emergencies are generally the only excusable form of absence for the final exam. In this case, we will set up a make-up exam.

chunks.title: Course Summary
chunks.content: MATH 311: Topics in Applied Math I is an introductory course in linear algebra with applications to multivariable calculus, offered in Spring 2026. This course is designed for students seeking to understand fundamental concepts in linear algebra and vector calculus. Students will learn to solve linear systems, calculate determinants, understand eigenvalues, classify critical points, parameterize curves and surfaces, and compute various integrals. Key theoretical skills include translating mathematical statements about vectors and matrices into abstract vector spaces and proving abstract statements. The course has no explicit prerequisites listed, suggesting it's accessible to students ready for an introductory college-level math course. Weekly homework, two midterms, and a final exam will assess learning.
